Title of Article or Chapter: The Maximal Covering Location Problem
Author(s): Richard L. Church; Charles ReVelle
Keywords: maximal covering; facility location; coverage; accessibility; network design; public service siting
200-word Summary: Canonical facility-location paper introducing the maximal covering location problem (MCLP), which selects a limited number of facilities to maximize covered demand within a service threshold. Although it predates EVs, the logic maps directly onto charging-station planning: with a limited budget, planners maximize coverage of population, trips, or vehicle flows subject to distance or travel-time limits. It is especially useful because many later EV siting papers are variations on covering models, sometimes adding flow, congestion, equity, or stochastic demand. For a national charging network, MCLP provides a baseline conceptual language for coverage, demand points, and service radius, and helps distinguish coverage-oriented design from cost-minimization or capacity-sizing models.
Relevance to Article: Foundational theory source for explaining why many EV charging problems are coverage problems and for motivating later extensions such as flow-capturing, congestion-aware, and multi-objective models.

Title of Article or Chapter: Location of Alternative-Fuel Stations Using the Flow-Refueling Location Model and Dispersion of Candidate Sites on Arcs
Author(s): Michael Kuby; Seow Lim
Keywords: FRLM; flow refueling; corridor charging; network flows; arc siting; alternative fuels; highway planning
200-word Summary: Extends the flow-refueling location model so stations can be placed not only at nodes but also along arcs, which is crucial when limited driving range makes vertex-only siting unrealistic. The model maximizes the amount of origin-destination flow that can complete round trips with refueling opportunities, making it especially relevant for intercity and national highway charging rather than purely urban charging. It is one of the most important bridges from classical location theory to transport-energy infrastructure because it treats charging as a network-feasibility problem rather than just a proximity problem.
Relevance to Article: Essential for a methodological framework that addresses highways, corridors, or nationwide route continuity; it justifies corridor-flow or OD-based models instead of purely population-based siting.

Title of Article or Chapter: Facility Location: Concepts, Models, Algorithms and Case Studies
Author(s): Reza Zanjirani Farahani; Masoud Hekmatfar
Keywords: facility location; optimization; p-median; p-center; covering; network design; algorithms
200-word Summary: Edited volume synthesizing major facility-location models, algorithms, and case studies across sectors. It is not EV-specific, but it gives the mathematical language behind almost every charging-location model used today: covering, median, center, fixed-charge, and multi-objective formulations. For an article on national charging siting, the book is useful because it helps classify EV papers correctly: some minimize user distance, some maximize covered traffic flow, some minimize cost, and others balance multiple criteria such as grid feasibility and equity.
Relevance to Article: Strong methodological background source for the theoretical section and for explaining why different objective functions lead to different charging-network designs.

Title of Article or Chapter: Multiple Criteria Facility Location Problems: A Survey
Author(s): Reza Zanjirani Farahani; Maryam SteadieSeifi; Nasrin Asgari
Keywords: multi-objective optimization; facility location; criteria weighting; trade-offs; cost; accessibility; sustainability
200-word Summary: Survey of bi-objective, multi-objective, and multi-attribute location problems that catalogs criteria commonly used in such models. Its value for EV charging is that charging infrastructure is rarely optimized on one criterion alone. National planners must simultaneously consider traffic demand, user detour, construction cost, grid capacity, land availability, environmental goals, and sometimes equity. The survey provides a rigorous framework for understanding those trade-offs and for justifying multi-criteria modeling choices.
Relevance to Article: Useful to support the argument that national EV charging is inherently multi-criteria and that transport, energy, economic, and social dimensions should be modeled together.

Name of Source: Transportation Research Part D: Transport and Environment
Title of Article or Chapter: Optimal Locations of Electric Public Charging Stations Using Real World Vehicle Travel Patterns
Author(s): Navid Shahraki; Hossein Cai; Min Xu; Mikhail Turkay
Keywords: travel patterns; public charging; demand estimation; real-world data; EV behavior; infrastructure planning
200-word Summary: Uses real-world vehicle travel patterns to infer charging demand and optimize public charging locations. Instead of assuming stylized or uniform demand, it builds location decisions from observed driving behavior, which is critical for national-scale planning where average-distance assumptions can misrepresent actual mobility. The paper is especially relevant if the article argues for empirical OD or trajectory data rather than purely demographic proxies.
Relevance to Article: Strong source for a data-driven demand-modeling section; supports using actual travel behavior rather than static population or administrative geography.

Name of Source: Transportation Research Part E: Logistics and Transportation Review
Title of Article or Chapter: The Battery Charging Station Location Problem: Impact of Users’ Range Anxiety and Distance Convenience
Author(s): Fang Guo; Jun Yang; Jianyi Lu
Keywords: range anxiety; user behavior; charging convenience; location model; behavioral optimization; demand sensitivity
200-word Summary: Explicitly incorporates range anxiety and travel convenience into the charging-station location problem. This is useful because charging infrastructure is not only a technical network-design issue but also a behavioral adoption issue. A nationally optimal network in engineering terms may still fail if users perceive it as too sparse or inconvenient. The paper helps bridge behavioral economics and operations research by showing that psychological constraints can change optimal siting decisions.
Relevance to Article: Useful for adding a behavioral dimension, especially if arguing that perceived accessibility and user confidence should be included alongside traffic-flow efficiency.

Title of Article or Chapter: Electric Vehicles Charging Infrastructure Location: A Genetic Algorithm Approach
Author(s): Dimitrios Efthymiou; Katerina Chrysostomou; Maria Morfoulaki; Georgia Aifantopoulou
Keywords: genetic algorithm; heuristic optimization; EV infrastructure; metaheuristics; siting; computational methods
200-word Summary: Applies a genetic algorithm to the EV charging infrastructure location problem. National-scale siting can become computationally difficult when candidate sites, demand points, constraints, and objectives all expand. Exact formulations may become expensive or infeasible, and metaheuristics offer a practical alternative. The paper shows how heuristic methods can search large solution spaces and still deliver meaningful planning insights.
Relevance to Article: Useful in a methods section to justify heuristic or metaheuristic approaches for country-scale charging design.

Title of Article or Chapter: Determination of Charging Infrastructure Location for Electric Vehicles
Author(s): Bálint Csonka; Csaba Csiszár
Keywords: multicriteria analysis; long-distance travel; urban demand; national roads; site screening; Hungary
200-word Summary: Proposes weighted multicriteria methods for both national roads and urban districts, explicitly distinguishing long-distance travel needs from local demand. It evaluates existing rest areas and strategic points such as border crossings and the capital, which is directly relevant if the article considers candidate-site generation along major roads.
Relevance to Article: Useful as a practical bridge between theory and policy, supporting a hybrid national framework where highways, border gateways, and urban demand centers are modeled differently.

Title of Article or Chapter: Planning Minimum Interurban Fast Charging Infrastructure for Electric Vehicles: Methodology and Application to Spain
Author(s): Antonio Colmenar-Santos; Carlos De Palacio; David Borge-Diez; Oscar Monzón-Alejandro
Keywords: Spain; interurban charging; fast charging; corridor planning; minimum infrastructure; national case study
200-word Summary: Spain-focused study developing a methodology for planning minimum interurban fast-charging infrastructure. It is valuable because it focuses on long-distance feasibility rather than purely urban convenience and provides an applied national case. It can help benchmark assumptions about spacing, network continuity, and the role of minimum viable coverage in early network deployment.
Relevance to Article: Essential if the article includes a European or Spanish angle; it provides a concrete national case study and precedent for interurban fast-charging optimization.

Title of Article or Chapter: A Location Model for Electric Vehicle (EV) Public Charging Stations Based on Drivers’ Existing Activities
Author(s): Long Pan; Enjian Yao; Yang Yang; Rui Zhang
Keywords: activity-based demand; charging choice; public charging; behavioral modeling; time budgets; urban mobility
200-word Summary: Proposes a location model that aims to preserve drivers’ existing activity patterns rather than forcing travel behavior to adapt to infrastructure. The model simulates charging choice while considering home charging availability, range anxiety, and the energy needs of remaining trips. It shows that charging-network design can be framed as minimizing lifestyle disruption, not merely minimizing distance or cost.
Relevance to Article: Useful for a behaviorally realistic demand section and for arguing that charging networks should be designed around how people actually move.

Name of Source: Transportation Research Part D: Transport and Environment
Title of Article or Chapter: Electric Vehicle Charging Station Locations: Elastic Demand, Station Congestion, and Network Equilibrium
Author(s): Yantao Huang; Kara Kockelman
Keywords: congestion; elastic demand; user equilibrium; queues; network equilibrium; pricing; travel assignment
200-word Summary: Models station locations under elastic charging demand, traffic congestion, charging queues, and network equilibrium. It shows that a sparse network can create not only access gaps but also severe congestion at strategically important stations. The paper endogenizes route choice, travel time, queueing, and user response, showing that station placement and station performance cannot be separated.
Relevance to Article: Useful for treating capacity and congestion explicitly and showing that where to place chargers and how much capacity to place there are linked decisions.

Title of Article or Chapter: Optimal Allocation of Electric Vehicle Charging Stations in a Highway Network: Part 1. Methodology and Test Application
Author(s): Giuseppe Napoli; Antonio Polimeni; Salvatore Micari; Laura Andaloro; Vincenzo Antonucci
Keywords: highway network; interurban charging; corridor design; methodology; highway demand; Italy
200-word Summary: Presents a highway-focused methodology for locating charging stations on a road network, emphasizing interurban mobility and practical corridor design. It treats highway charging as a system that must support longer trips, range constraints, and strategic corridor continuity.
Relevance to Article: Excellent for a case-study and methodology comparison section, offering a concrete highway-network framework that can be contrasted with urban and activity-based models.

Name of Source: Transportation Research Part A: Policy and Practice
Title of Article or Chapter: The Spatial Planning of Public Electric Vehicle Charging Infrastructure in a High-Density City Using a Contextualised Location-Allocation Model
Author(s): Sylvia Y. He; Yong-Hong Kuo; Ka Kit Sun
Keywords: location-allocation; policy constraints; projected demand; high-density city; budget sensitivity; charger expansion
200-word Summary: Combines policy constraints, survey-based EV purchase intention, projected charging demand, and a location-allocation model. Its broader contribution is methodological: it contextualizes optimization within actual planning rules and budget scenarios, rather than treating siting as a purely abstract mathematical problem.
Relevance to Article: Useful to show how optimization should be contextualized by policy, budget, and site constraints.

Title of Article or Chapter: Charging Station Location and Sizing for Electric Vehicles Under Congestion
Author(s): Ömer Burak Kınay; Fatma Gzara; Sibel A. Alumur
Keywords: location and sizing; congestion; stochastic flows; capacity planning; service levels; queueing
200-word Summary: Integrates location and capacity sizing under congestion and stochastic EV flows. Many siting studies stop at location choice, while real national infrastructure planning must decide both where and how large stations should be. The paper therefore closes a gap between corridor coverage models and operational realism.
Relevance to Article: Strong source for the capacity-sizing section and for arguing that country-level optimization should treat charger numbers and station throughput as endogenous.

Title of Article or Chapter: Optimizing Electric Vehicle Charging Station Location on Highways: A Decision Model for Meeting Intercity Travel Demand
Author(s): Ibrahim Tumay Gulbahar; Muhammed Sutcu; Abedalmuhdi Almomany; Babul Salam KSM Kader Ibrahim
Keywords: highways; intercity demand; EV forecasting; charger numbers; corridor siting; Türkiye
200-word Summary: Targets optimal placement of charging stations along highways to meet intercity travel demand and estimates future EV numbers as inputs to the siting model. It is useful because it links infrastructure planning to fleet growth instead of assuming today’s demand is enough.
Relevance to Article: Useful as a direct analogue for country-scale road-network planning, especially if integrating demand forecasting with corridor optimization.

Title of Article or Chapter: Optimal Location of Electric Vehicle Charging Stations Using Proximity Diagrams
Author(s): Carmen Calvo-Jurado; José María Ceballos-Martínez; José Carlos García-Merino; Marina Muñoz-Solano; Fernando Jesús Sánchez-Herrera
Keywords: Voronoi diagrams; GIS; spatial optimization; proximity analysis; network expansion; geometric methods
200-word Summary: Proposes a geometric method based on Voronoi/proximity diagrams to identify optimal new charging locations relative to existing infrastructure. It is a useful complement to optimization papers because it offers a spatial-analytic heuristic for identifying underserved areas and candidate expansion zones.
Relevance to Article: Useful for GIS and exploratory spatial analysis, supporting a two-stage workflow: identify underserved regions geometrically, then optimize within them.

Name of Source: Central European Journal of Operations Research
Title of Article or Chapter: Advanced Optimization Models for the Location of Charging Stations in E-Mobility
Author(s): Radek Staněk; Petr Buzna
Keywords: deterministic flow refuelling; construction cost; policy implications; objective extensions; operations research; e-mobility
200-word Summary: Extends deterministic flow-refuelling models with new objectives and constraints, emphasizing that location-dependent construction costs can materially change the charging infrastructure selected. For national planning, this matters because rural, mountainous, or grid-constrained areas often have very different deployment costs.
Relevance to Article: Useful to justify a cost-sensitive national model and to explain why traffic-maximizing and budget-efficient solutions may diverge.

Name of Source: Transportation Research Part C: Emerging Technologies
Title of Article or Chapter: Charging Station Location Problem: A Comprehensive Review on Models and Solution Approaches
Author(s): Mouna Kchaou-Boujelben
Keywords: literature review; solution methods; models; charging-station siting; optimization review; taxonomy
200-word Summary: Comprehensive review synthesizing the charging-station location literature and classifying both model types and solution approaches. It provides a clear taxonomy: deterministic vs stochastic, node-based vs flow-based, exact vs heuristic, static vs dynamic, and more.
Relevance to Article: Essential for organizing the literature review and identifying open gaps, especially for national-scale, interurban, and integrated energy-transport models.

Title of Article or Chapter: Toward Sustainable Transportation: A Systematic Review of EV Charging Station Locations
Author(s): Moses Katontoka; Francesco Orsi; Martha Bakker; Boris Hocks
Keywords: systematic review; EVCS locations; siting criteria; evidence synthesis; planning; sustainability
200-word Summary: Recent systematic review asking what patterns and dominant criteria emerge from the growing literature on EV charging-site selection. It helps identify consensus versus fragmentation across methods and criteria.
Relevance to Article: Useful as a recent review anchor for framing the state of the art and explaining why national-scale synthesis remains needed.

Title of Article or Chapter: The 2030 National Charging Network: Estimating U.S. Light-Duty Demand for Electric Vehicle Charging Infrastructure
Author(s): Eric Wood; Brennan Borlaug; Matt Moniot; Dong-Yeon Lee; Yanbo Ge; Fan Yang; Zhaocai Liu
Keywords: national network; charging demand; scenario modeling; U.S.; infrastructure planning; EVI-X; corridors
200-word Summary: Policy-relevant national planning document using modular scenario-based modeling to estimate light-duty charging needs for 2030 and treating different charging use cases separately. It demonstrates how to translate EV adoption scenarios into a national charging-network buildout rather than merely optimizing a fixed current demand map.
Relevance to Article: Excellent for a national planning and scenario-analysis section; shows how to connect optimization with policy-relevant infrastructure projections.

Title of Article or Chapter: National Plug-In Electric Vehicle Infrastructure Analysis
Author(s): Matteo Muratori; Eric Wood; Clement Rames; Marc Melaina
Keywords: corridor charging; urban-rural balance; infrastructure roadmap; nationwide minimum coverage; co-evolution
200-word Summary: Distinguishes charging needs along interstate corridors from those within urban and rural communities. It argues that a relatively limited number of corridor fast-charging stations may enable long-distance travel between cities, while broader community charging requires much larger infrastructure. This distinction is crucial for national optimization papers.
Relevance to Article: Useful to clarify the difference between minimum corridor coverage and full national accessibility, supporting a layered national strategy.

Title of Article or Chapter: Global EV Outlook 2025
Author(s): International Energy Agency
Keywords: EV adoption; global charging infrastructure; policy trends; battery demand; scenario outlooks; public charging
200-word Summary: Provides macro context on EV deployment, battery demand, and charging infrastructure worldwide. It is valuable not as a siting model but as strategic context, showing the pace of market growth, the scale of infrastructure expansion, and the policy environment in which national charging networks are being planned.
Relevance to Article: Best for the introduction and policy-context sections, providing up-to-date evidence on why national charging optimization matters.

Title of Article or Chapter: Regulation (EU) 2023/1804 on the Deployment of Alternative Fuels Infrastructure (AFIR)
Author(s): European Parliament; Council of the European Union
Keywords: AFIR; TEN-T; corridor standards; fast charging; compliance; EU policy; minimum spacing
200-word Summary: Major European policy text for national charging planning. It sets requirements for publicly accessible recharging pools for light-duty EVs along the TEN-T core and comprehensive networks, including spacing and aggregate power thresholds that rise over time.
Relevance to Article: Critical for any article with a European policy dimension; can serve as an external benchmark, constraint set, or validation criterion for a national optimization model.

Title of Article or Chapter: Equity Considerations in Public Electric Vehicle Charging: A Review
Author(s): Boyou Chen; Austin Moore; Bochen Jia; Kaihan Zhang; Mengqiu Cao
Keywords: equity; accessibility; justice; public charging; demographics; inclusive planning; spatial inequality
200-word Summary: Addresses a dimension often missing from purely efficiency-driven siting: equity. It argues that networks optimized only for traffic or profitability may underserve low-income regions, renters, peripheral communities, or places without home charging.
Relevance to Article: Useful for adding a normative and social-justice perspective, especially if critiquing purely market- or traffic-based optimization.
